RLab 05 – Simple Linear Regression Model

Roshan Thapa Magar

2025-10-20

Introduction

This lab explores simple linear regression using the Salaries dataset from the carData package. We’ll examine relationships between faculty salaries and various predictors, including years since PhD, years of service, rank, discipline, and sex.

Question 0

This question comes from exercises in section 23.2.1: One challenge with performing numerical optimization is that it’s only guaranteed to find one local optimum. What’s the problem with optimizing a three parameter model like this?

model1 <- function(a, data) {
  a[1] + data$x * a[2] + a[3]
}

Answer to Question 0

This model has too many parameters. Linear regression only needs two (intercept and slope), but this has three. Both a[1] and a[3] are constant terms, creating redundancy. The computer can’t find the best values because there are infinitely many combinations that work.

Data Exploration

library(carData)
library(tidyverse)
library(modelr)
head(Salaries)
##        rank discipline yrs.since.phd yrs.service  sex salary
## 1      Prof          B            19          18 Male 139750
## 2      Prof          B            20          16 Male 173200
## 3  AsstProf          B             4           3 Male  79750
## 4      Prof          B            45          39 Male 115000
## 5      Prof          B            40          41 Male 141500
## 6 AssocProf          B             6           6 Male  97000

The dataset contains 397 faculty members with variables: - rank: Academic rank (AsstProf, AssocProf, Prof) - discipline: Field of study (A or B) - yrs.since.phd: Years since PhD - yrs.service: Years of service - sex: Gender (Male, Female) - salary: Annual salary in dollars

Scatterplots to Explore Data Sets

Scatterplots with the pairs Command

pairs(Salaries)

The pairs plot shows relationships between all variables. Key observations: - Strong positive correlation between yrs.since.phd and yrs.service - Clear salary differences by rank - Potential gender differences in salary

Scatterplots with ggplot

Salary vs Years Since PhD

Salaries %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=yrs.since.phd, y=salary)) +
  stat_smooth(mapping=aes(x=yrs.since.phd, y=salary), method="lm") +
  labs(title="Salary vs Years Since PhD",
       x="Years Since PhD", 
       y="Salary ($)")
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Salary vs Years of Service

Salaries %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=yrs.service, y=salary)) +
  stat_smooth(mapping=aes(x=yrs.service, y=salary), method="lm") +
  labs(title="Salary vs Years of Service",
       x="Years of Service", 
       y="Salary ($)")
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Salary by Categorical Variables

Salaries %>%
  pivot_longer(c(rank, discipline, sex), names_to="xvar", values_to="xvalue") %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_boxplot(mapping=aes(x=xvalue, y=salary)) +
  facet_wrap(facets="xvar", scales="free_x") +
  labs(title="Salary by Categorical Variables",
       x="Variable", 
       y="Salary ($)")

Gender Differences in Salary vs Years Since PhD

Salaries %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=yrs.since.phd, y=salary, color=sex)) +
  stat_smooth(mapping=aes(x=yrs.since.phd, y=salary, color=sex), method="lm") +
  labs(title="Salary vs Years Since PhD by Gender",
       x="Years Since PhD", 
       y="Salary ($)",
       color="Gender")
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Question 1 Analysis

a) Strong positive relationship between years of service and years since PhD. More years since PhD means more years of service since people start working after their degree.

b) Sharp edge exists because you can’t have more years of service than years since PhD. All points stay on or below the diagonal line.

c) Years since PhD has stronger connection to salary than years of service. The salary vs years since PhD plot shows a clearer straight line pattern.

d) Least helpful graph shows categorical variables like rank vs discipline. These are just categories, so plotting them against each other doesn’t show relationships.

Question 2: Gender Analysis (Limited to <40 years since PhD)

Salaries %>%
  filter(yrs.since.phd < 40) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=yrs.since.phd, y=salary, color=sex)) +
  stat_smooth(mapping=aes(x=yrs.since.phd, y=salary, color=sex), method="lm") +
  labs(title="Salary vs Years Since PhD by Gender (<40 years since PhD)",
       x="Years Since PhD", 
       y="Salary ($)",
       color="Gender")
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

a) After limiting to faculty with <40 years since PhD, there is a pay difference. Women (red line) consistently make less than men (blue line) with similar experience.

b) The gray shaded areas don’t overlap much, especially in middle/higher ranges. This means the pay difference is real, not random chance.

The Linear Correlation Coefficient

# Create correlation examples
df = data.frame(x=c(-2:2, rnorm(30), -2:2), 
                y=c(2:(-2), rnorm(30), -2:2),
                r = c(rep("r=-1", 5), rep("r=0", 30), rep("r=1", 5)))
ggplot(data=df) +
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=x, y=y)) +
  facet_wrap(facets="r") +
  labs(title="Examples of Different Correlation Coefficients")

Correlation Analysis

cor(Salaries$salary, Salaries$yrs.since.phd)
## [1] 0.4192311
Salaries %>% 
  select(salary, yrs.since.phd) %>% 
  cor()
##                  salary yrs.since.phd
## salary        1.0000000     0.4192311
## yrs.since.phd 0.4192311     1.0000000
Salaries %>% 
  select(salary, yrs.since.phd, yrs.service) %>% 
  cor()
##                  salary yrs.since.phd yrs.service
## salary        1.0000000     0.4192311   0.3347447
## yrs.since.phd 0.4192311     1.0000000   0.9096491
## yrs.service   0.3347447     0.9096491   1.0000000

Question 3 Analysis

Yes, the correlations match what I saw visually. The correlation between salary and years since PhD is 0.42, while salary and years of service is 0.40. Years since PhD has slightly higher correlation, matching the visual observation that it looked more like a straight line.

Regression Analysis

Simple Linear Regression: Salary vs Years Since PhD

salary_mod <- lm(salary ~ yrs.since.phd, data=Salaries)
summary(salary_mod)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = salary ~ yrs.since.phd, data = Salaries)
## 
## Residuals:
##    Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
## -84171 -19432  -2858  16086 102383 
## 
## Coefficients:
##               Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)    91718.7     2765.8  33.162   <2e-16 ***
## yrs.since.phd    985.3      107.4   9.177   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 27530 on 395 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.1758, Adjusted R-squared:  0.1737 
## F-statistic: 84.23 on 1 and 395 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
coef(salary_mod)
##   (Intercept) yrs.since.phd 
##    91718.6854      985.3421

Model Equation: Salary = 91,718.69 + 985.34 × Years Since PhD

  • R² = 0.176: The model explains 17.6% of salary variation
  • p-value < 2.2e-16: Highly significant relationship
  • Residual SE = 27,530: Average prediction error

Visualizing Predictions and Residuals

head(Salaries %>% add_predictions(salary_mod))
##        rank discipline yrs.since.phd yrs.service  sex salary      pred
## 1      Prof          B            19          18 Male 139750 110440.19
## 2      Prof          B            20          16 Male 173200 111425.53
## 3  AsstProf          B             4           3 Male  79750  95660.05
## 4      Prof          B            45          39 Male 115000 136059.08
## 5      Prof          B            40          41 Male 141500 131132.37
## 6 AssocProf          B             6           6 Male  97000  97630.74
Salaries %>% 
  add_predictions(salary_mod) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=yrs.since.phd, y=salary)) +
  geom_line(mapping=aes(x=yrs.since.phd, y=pred), color="red", size=1) +
  labs(title="Salary vs Years Since PhD with Linear Model",
       x="Years Since PhD", 
       y="Salary ($)")
## Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
## ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.
## This warning is displayed once every 8 hours.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.

Salaries %>% 
  add_residuals(salary_mod) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x=yrs.since.phd, y=resid)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color="red", linetype="dashed") +
  labs(title="Residuals vs Years Since PhD",
       x="Years Since PhD", 
       y="Residuals ($)")

The residual plot shows some curvature, suggesting the linear model may not capture all patterns in the data.

Categorical Predictors: Salary vs Rank

salary_mod2 <- lm(salary ~ rank, data=Salaries)
coef(salary_mod2)
##   (Intercept) rankAssocProf      rankProf 
##      80775.99      13100.45      45996.12

Model Interpretation: - Assistant Professor (base): $80,776 - Associate Professor: $80,776 + $13,100 = $93,876 - Full Professor: $80,776 + $45,996 = $126,772

Salaries %>% 
  add_predictions(salary_mod2) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x=rank, y=salary)) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x=rank, y=pred), color="red", size=3) +
  labs(title="Salary by Rank with Model Predictions",
       x="Academic Rank", 
       y="Salary ($)")

Question 4: Salary vs Years of Service

salary_mod_service <- lm(salary ~ yrs.service, data=Salaries)
summary(salary_mod_service)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = salary ~ yrs.service, data = Salaries)
## 
## Residuals:
##    Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
## -81933 -20511  -3776  16417 101947 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  99974.7     2416.6   41.37  < 2e-16 ***
## yrs.service    779.6      110.4    7.06 7.53e-12 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 28580 on 395 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.1121, Adjusted R-squared:  0.1098 
## F-statistic: 49.85 on 1 and 395 DF,  p-value: 7.529e-12
coef(salary_mod_service)
## (Intercept) yrs.service 
##  99974.6529    779.5691

Model Equation: Salary = 99,974.65 + 779.57 × Years of Service

  • R² = 0.112: Only explains 11.2% of salary variation
  • p-value = 7.53e-12: Statistically significant but weak relationship
Salaries %>% 
  add_predictions(salary_mod_service) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=yrs.service, y=salary)) +
  geom_line(mapping=aes(x=yrs.service, y=pred), color="red", size=1) +
  labs(title="Salary vs Years of Service with Linear Model",
       x="Years of Service", 
       y="Salary ($)")

Salaries %>% 
  add_residuals(salary_mod_service) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x=yrs.service, y=resid)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color="red", linetype="dashed") +
  labs(title="Residuals vs Years of Service",
       x="Years of Service", 
       y="Residuals ($)")

Question 4 Analysis

The linear fit is not good. The residual plot shows a curved pattern instead of random dots around zero. The model over-predicts for people with few/many years of service but under-predicts for those in the middle range. We need a curved model, not a straight line.

Summary and Conclusions

Key Findings

  1. Years Since PhD vs Years of Service: Strong positive correlation (r = 0.91), with a clear diagonal boundary due to logical constraints.

  2. Salary Relationships:

    • Years Since PhD: Moderate positive correlation (r = 0.42), explains 17.6% of salary variation
    • Years of Service: Weaker positive correlation (r = 0.33), explains only 11.2% of salary variation
    • Academic Rank: Strongest predictor with clear salary differences between ranks

  3. Gender Differences: After controlling for years since PhD (<40 years), women consistently earn less than men with similar experience.

  4. Model Quality:

    • Years Since PhD model: Reasonable fit with some curvature in residuals
    • Years of Service model: Poor fit with clear non-linear patterns in residuals

Statistical Insights

  • The relationship between salary and years of service appears non-linear, suggesting diminishing returns or other complex patterns
  • Years since PhD provides better linear prediction than years of service
  • Academic rank shows the clearest salary stratification
  • Gender pay differences persist even after controlling for experience

Recommendations

  1. Consider non-linear models for years of service (polynomial or spline regression)
  2. Investigate additional predictors that might explain salary variation
  3. Further examine gender pay differences with more sophisticated models
  4. Consider interaction effects between variables (e.g., rank × years since PhD)

This analysis demonstrates the importance of both visual exploration and statistical modeling in understanding complex relationships in salary data.
